summary: Paul Graham argues that despite the excesses of the Internet Bubble, it contained real truths that will matter more over time than the mistakes. He claims the internet’s real impact is expanding choice, rewarding substance over marketing, and amplifying productivity, while shifting power toward young, technically adept founders and startup-driven innovation.
key_points: Early public markets for pre-earnings companies are a form of retail VC and can be rational if markets learn to value them well.
The internet’s biggest economic gains often come indirectly, not from the most “Internettish” companies.
Lower distribution costs and faster word-of-mouth make “build it, and they will come” more viable, rewarding quality over bottlenecks.
Nerd culture and informality rise because technical substance matters and is increasingly tied to wealth creation.
Stock options and startups align incentives with value creation, though options may need better alignment with real value.
money_quotes: Over the long term, what the Bubble got right will be more important than what it got wrong.
Recognizing an important trend turns out to be easier than figuring out how to profit from it.
The exciting thing about the Internet is that it's shifting everything in that direction.
Dressing up is inevitably a substitute for good ideas.
Technology is a lever.  It doesn't add; it multiplies.
key_insight: The bubble’s real legacy is a long-term shift toward markets and cultures that reward good ideas, technical substance, and startup-driven innovation more directly than before.
